findings.description: Email/SMTP header injection (CWE-93/CWE-80): _render() interpolates attacker-controllable msg.to and msg.subject directly into a hand-built RFC822 header block separated by \r\n, so a CRLF in either field injects arbitrary headers (Bcc/Cc/Reply-To) or splits in a forged body; output flows to sendmail() in flush().

The To/Subject header lines built with raw f-string \r\n separators perform no CRLF sanitization or header encoding; the imported-but-unused MIMEText (line 6) shows the safe email API was abandoned, leaving the header block injectable.

Cleartext SMTP transmission (CWE-319): flush() opens a plain smtplib.SMTP connection on submission port 587 and never calls starttls(), so message contents (potentially sensitive bodies) traverse the network unencrypted.
No SMTP authentication: server.login() is never called before sendmail(), so the queue relies solely on an unauthenticated open relay on the internal host, broadening abuse if that host is reachable.

Envelope-recipient injection: msg.to is passed unvalidated as sendmail() to_addrs; while smtplib escapes SMTP commands, an unvalidated recipient string combined with the header-injection root cause widens the CRLF attack surface.
Dedup key is only msg.to, so a second, genuinely different message to the same recipient is silently skipped/dropped (correctness bug with a minor availability angle); defer detail to logic critic.
Message.template is declared but never referenced in _render(); dead field with no effect; defer to logic critic.
flush() opens a new SMTP connection per message inside the loop instead of reusing one connection, adding connection/TLS overhead per send; defer detail to performance critic.

_pending and _seen grow without bound and _seen is never trimmed except via clear_dedup_cache(), so an actor able to enqueue many distinct recipients could drive unbounded memory growth (low-confidence memory DoS).
